title: The Drone Threat to America’s Cities
gist: Cheap, mass-produced FPV and Shahed drones—proven asymmetrically lethal in Ukraine and used by Iran, ISIS, and Hezbollah—pose a serious and underappreciated threat to U.S. cities and critical infrastructure, and U.S. counter-drone defenses are nearly nonexistent because of legal restrictions, detection limits, and countermeasures (fiber-optic and AI-piloted drones) that defeat jamming.

themes.theme: Asymmetric drone warfare proven in Ukraine

insights.claim: Ukraine has fought Russia to a standstill largely by mass-producing cheap drones—4 million FPV drones last year and an expected 7 million this year—assembled from commercial and 3D-printed components in hundreds of nondescript buildings, inflicting over 1.3 million Russian casualties.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Cheap drones have rewritten the cost-exchange ratio of warfare—structural demand for counter-UAS systems and drone-component supply chains; watch defense budgets pivot here.
insights.quote: Last year, Ukraine produced 4 million FPVs, and it expects to make another 7 million this year.
insights.locator: In Ukraine, most of the carnage on the battlefield

insights.claim: Cheap mass-produced FPV drones have transformed 21st-century warfare and terrorist groups have taken notice—ISIS pioneered armed drones in the 2016-2017 Mosul fight and Hezbollah has used FPV drones against Israeli troops in Lebanon.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: The drone playbook has already proliferated to non-state actors, making the homeland-attack scenario a near-term rather than speculative threat.
insights.quote: ISIS pioneered the use of cheap armed drones during the Iraqi fight to retake Mosul in 2016-2017
insights.locator: These cheap, mass-produced drones have transformed 21st century warfare

insights.claim: A few dozen Iranian Shahed drones launched from fishing trawlers 15 miles offshore could hit three of Los Angeles County's four oil refineries within about 10 minutes at 115 mph, flying below radar, with neither the coastline nor refineries defended by interceptors.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Coastal energy and port infrastructure are undefended soft targets; a successful strike would cause fuel shortages and economic damage—a tail risk for refinery operators and regional energy supply.
insights.quote: three of Los Angeles County's four oil refineries—in about 10 minutes
insights.locator: But make no mistake, a few dozen Shaheds

insights.claim: The Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach—where 40 percent of all U.S. oceangoing containerized trade enters—plus LAX, the power grid, and Gulf Coast oil-and-gas infrastructure are all soft targets vulnerable to drone attack.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: A drone strike on these chokepoints would ripple through supply chains and energy markets; concentration of trade through LA/Long Beach is a systemic vulnerability.
insights.quote: the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach, where 40 percent of all oceangoing containerized trade enters the U.S.
insights.locator: And oil refineries aren't the only infrastructure

insights.claim: There are over 837,000 registered drones in the U.S. with at least 12,500 in LA County alone, and because registration enforcement is minimal and small drones need no registration, authorities cannot distinguish a hobbyist flight from a lethal threat before the final seconds.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: The sheer airspace noise makes broad-area detection impractical, capping the addressable market for fixed C-UAS to discrete high-value sites rather than city-wide coverage.
insights.quote: there are over 837,000 registered drones in the United States, with at least 12,500 in Los Angeles County alone
insights.locator: Think about the sheer volume of “noise” in the skies

insights.claim: Digital ID rules require drones to broadcast only by radio frequency, not cellular internet, inherently limiting tracking range, so a city-wide RF sensor network would be extraordinarily expensive and practically useless—C-UAS is deployed only to protect discrete sites like airports or the Super Bowl.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Schools, parks, and outdoor malls cannot be economically protected with current detection tech—a coverage gap that defines where counter-UAS innovation must go.
insights.quote: the rules only require broadcasting by radio frequency (RF)—not cellular internet—which inherently limits the tracking range
insights.locator: For one, the U.S. doesn't track all the drones in the sky

themes.theme: Jamming is fighting the last war

insights.claim: Drone tactics are outgrowing jammable radio frequencies: both sides in Ukraine tether FPV drones via ultrathin fiber-optic cable up to 20 km (Hezbollah uses this against Israel, which 'has so far been unable to develop any effective countermeasures'), and fully autonomous AI-piloted drones need no comms link, so 'no signal means there's nothing to jam.'
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Electronic-jamming counter-drone solutions are being obsoleted; the durable counter-UAS market is in kinetic/physical and AI-detection approaches, not RF jamming.
insights.quote: No signal means there's nothing to jam. In short, the focus on how to electronically jam drones may be fighting the last war.
insights.locator: While electronic jamming or geofencing might work

insights.claim: State and local authorities were legally barred from using electronic jamming until Congress changed the law in late 2025 via the SAFER SKIES Act, and even now cannot jam until trained and certified by the DOJ under regulations not yet finalized—while private infrastructure owners remain banned.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Even available countermeasures cannot be deployed due to certification bottlenecks—a regulatory drag that delays the counter-UAS market and leaves a near-term defensive vacuum.
insights.quote: state and local authorities were strictly prohibited under federal law from using electronic jamming equipment until late 2025, when Congress finally changed the law
insights.locator: But even if the FPV drones could be brought down

insights.claim: Officials responsible for major-event security warn the threat is real and capabilities are absent—the LA28 Olympics security chief called drones an overarching concern, and the FIFA World Cup 2026 safety officer said 'we don't have the training, we don't have the equipment, and we don't have the number of personnel.'
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Upcoming mega-events (World Cup 2026, LA28) are acknowledged-vulnerable, near-term catalysts likely to force urgent counter-UAS spending.
insights.quote: We don't have the training, we don't have the equipment, and we don't have the number of personnel that are skilled in mitigation technology
insights.locator: In sum and as G.B. Jones

insights.claim: The author predicts U.S. and European cities will start draping outdoor malls, venues, and streets with anti-drone netting cut from fishing nets—an aesthetic the author calls tragic—until faster federal certifications, scalable detection, and post-jamming innovation arrive.
insights.speaker: Seth Stodder
insights.so_what: Crude physical netting may become the insurer- and lawyer-demanded default protective measure—an early, low-tech market signal for the counter-drone gap.
insights.quote: draped with anti-drone netting cut from fishing nets, installed to ensnare quadcopter drone blades
insights.locator: In the meantime, and until we figure out a better answer
